speaker: interviewer
text: --1995, tape 4. Mr. Hirsch, I'd like to concentrate a little bit and talk about the barracks themselves. How many people shared these barracks with you? And were they Jews and non-Jews? Was it a mix of people? How many people were in there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each barracks-- each barrack might have had 300 to 400 people. There were three layers of bunk beds. And they were all, in my barrack, all Jews. The way I understood it, towards the end of the camp-- homosexuals, political prisoners. There no-- there were no women at the time in the camp. We did have, as I mentioned before, a gentile barrack supervisor. But that was it. Inspections were done by uniformed SS men periodically for cleanliness, staying at attention, toothbrush inspection. And our contact was strictly within our own barrack. There were-- what, when we were ordered to march, we'd march in groups. When we ate, we ate-- or rather they, I was the food carrier-- ate at the same standing-up position at that table or crouching down somewheres. So each barrack had its own sanitary facilities. And there was very little contact between barracks. But within the barrack-- we had, unfortunately, an epileptic in our barrack. So that was a miserable situation at times. But we made friends within our barracks. There were other students. There were some people that I knew, merchants my father had done business with, two brothers, [NON-ENGLISH], a Hungarian name, probably at one time [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a gentleman that lived in our building. He was in the carpentry business. As the winter progressed, we were issued some tattered military overcoats. When I finally was called out, I gave him my overcoat. He wasn't issued one. It was totally random and haphazard. The only punishment I ever received was I was standing in line to purchase these sardines. And I had my hand in my pocket. An SS man kicked me in my behind with such force that I really almost collapsed and shouted, take your hand out! I didn't know there was such a regulation. It made no difference to me. But that was really the only punishment other than general punishment-- standing at attention, marching, individual punishment. It was the only one-- only one that I ever encountered personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you about Dachau being a labor-- a forced-labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you become a food carrier? And, and, and what happened to the other-- to the other inmates? What kinds of jobs did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just picked by my supervisor. He obviously looked at my size and my build, and he lined me up together with some other young men. And we were the food carriers. Other times, we had to march continuously in exercise. We used shovels in lieu of guns to continuously exercise and march all day long-- run in unison, march, use shovels as simulated gun to do-- That gives you an idea that the supervisors were actually militarily oriented. And that's all they knew what to do with us. We were not digging ditches. We were not transporting rocks from one side of the street to the other, or anything like that. All that must have come later. I'll be honest with you. There are certain experiences that I had-- that I carry with me all my life. People were able to buy cigarettes. And those who had the money, they smoked and smoked them to the end, holding it in a characteristic way this way. And then they would throw them away. And they would be followed by two, three other fellows who didn't have the money-- parents didn't or relatives. And they would then get into a brawl to pick up this quarter-inch long piece that the other chap had thrown away, so that they would roll again their own cigarettes out of whatever paper they could get a hold of. And when I saw this fighting over this little quarter-inch piece of a cigarette, I started to understand human nature better, and addiction, and then decided that I'd never smoke. And when I saw these pogroms, if you wish, in Poland, I also decided that, when it came to drinking, it wasn't going to my-- one of my weaknesses. I admit, I'll have a glass of wine with dinner. But that is about all. And so those two things stuck in my mind. And I decided it's just not for me. But the episode with cigarettes is so vivid in my mind that I wanted to bring it out. Many other people came to me and said, we saw you help these older people. You saved their lives. I never looked at it that way. I thought it was a natural thing to do. And some of these older people could have been my parents-- my father. There were only men. And so I just pitched in as best as I could. In retrospect, it might have been a foolish thing to do. But as it was, it worked out. And time went by. And one nice day, my name was called during the major assembly. And I was taken to the main office. And I was handed a small brown envelope and my clothing in a bundle. And in this envelope was my bar mitzvah watch, some other little personal things-- some kind of a thin, gold ring that I had gotten from a relative, the things that were taken from me when I was taken in there. And it should give an insight into the German mentality of correctness and orderliness. Even under those circumstances, everything had to be according to the book. It was only later, I presume, that they stole. And because the way I see this whole thing was that, as times become-- became worse for the Germans, like food became scarce. And the war didn't go so well. And to feed all these Jews, et cetera became a major-- call it drag, if you wish-- on their economic well-being. They decided this can't continue like this. The world doesn't accept them anyway. We got to get rid of them. That's the way I see it. I don't think it was originally meant to go in to the cremation camps that I've personally seen and have photographs of. Because I think that it evolves like everything else, almost like a relationship. It started out-- in-- now that I look back, I'm tempted to say in a benign way. Just get them in to a camp, treat them like soldiers. Just keep them away from the German society, so they don't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] infiltrate their mentality to the German thinking. That was his main concern. I also want to bring out that the way I see this whole structure of Hitler and his animosity towards the Jews was not religious at all. He couldn't care less if the Jews killed Christ because he really had no sympathy for Christ's philosophy to turn your other cheek. And so that was not his thought. His thought was banging both cheeks if you can. And so his idea was the Jews were internationalists. The Jews had a mentality which transcended the national border. Even though they were good citizens-- they served in the Army. My wife will show the honor roll that her father got from the Austrian Empire as being a lieutenant in the Austrian Army, and all this. But the Nazis felt that they were internationalists. They had relatives all over the world to whom they could write what Germany is trying to do to get again into the arms race. And he wanted to prevent that. He wanted to get rid of the Jews so that he could keep everything quiet, which he did until he burst into France. Let us face it. He had the Germans exercise with wooden rifles, with wooden cars and tanks before he built them. By the time he got strong enough to build them, it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: You asked us why we didn't resist. I'm asking you, why didn't England, and Czechoslovakia, and Poland not resist? Life isn't that simple. Surely when you are in the Warsaw Ghetto, when they were up against it, and when there was no end in sight, they had no choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in Dachau-- when I was in Dachau, the thought permeated all of us. Hey, we applied for visas. Comes the day they're going to let-- nobody thought of that we may be sent from Dachau, as it later happened, to other camps, and from there to the final destination, like it happened to my parents. That thought didn't come to our mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, when you were in Dachau, you didn't-- you-- did you witness beatings, murder every day? Or was it just like a military camp, and if you stayed in line, you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Precisely. And some people hanged themselves in the toilet because they couldn't cope. Was it lack of family? Was it the situation, this very thing staying in line, that they couldn't cope with? Or their own personal makeup, their sensitivity, or their stress level? They couldn't cope. They hung themselves. But there were no overt murders. Beatings only took place when somebody crossed the line. Somebody, as I said before, stuffed-- it was strictly announced-- no newspapers in your clothing. They knew that this was going to be done. And those who did it anyway they were found out. They were beaten, brutally beaten, I believe, to set an example. It's almost like, you have several children, one misbehaves. You try to set an example, modern education notwithstanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you believe that the inmates taken to Dachau at the time were taken on a temporary basis, and the intention was to release them--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: --at a given point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must tell you. I had no special pull. And I'll illustrate this. When, when I was taken to Dachau, my mother wrote a letter to this senator in the United States, saying that her son--

speaker: interviewer
text: Senator Sabbath? That was Senator Sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Senator Sabbath. We have a copy of the letter-- I don't have it anymore-- that Senator Sabbath sent to the Austrian-- to the United States delegation in Austria. And I'm quoting as much as I can remember. My distant relative, Charles Hirsch, has been taken to Dachau. I want you to do for him to free him, whatever you can, within the framework of the law.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the phraseology used. Now you know what that meant. What was the man supposed to do? I was no Einstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, and so there was no special treatment that I was called. I was called because my visa was issued. And they wanted to get rid of me. They knew I was emigrate-- I would emigrate. And I came home. And I had to register.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get home?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took you home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got a ticket, went home-- unsupervised.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were released from the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken to the train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got on the train-- by one of the SS men, period. What? You couldn't escape. You couldn't go any place. You had no identification cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, if you wanted to do it like in the movies, that was something else. Or like this chap now did in Nepal, or wherever he was. But in those days, that wasn't the mentality. I'm going home. I have a visa. Correct? That was the mindset.

speaker: interviewee
text: Furthermore, Dachau was in Germany. My accent was different. I would have been found out very quickly. Regardless, I had a visa. I went home to my parents, my brother, and sister. I didn't know if they also had a visa. It came like a bolt out of the clear blue. And I came home. And I had to register with the police every day. Every day, I had to go to police station-- to answer your question-- that I'm here. And one day, I crossed a huge square in Vienna against the light-- I just want to bring to the scene as a humorous situation. And a policeman called me over. And I said, oh my god, no, I just came out of there. And he gave me a warning and let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things, little things like that stay in the mind, the fear that you have momentarily. What's this now? What-- what's going to happen here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd been for the examination. We prepared ourselves for the trip. My mother had prepared for us clothing and God knows what. And one little detail is in Europe-- the shirts don't open all the way down so they can go through the automatic manglers in the United States. So all these shirts were pretty much difficult to have laundered here, et cetera. But she had prepared. I guess she stuck her last penny into this. And then they came to the apartment. And they had all the trunks emptied, down into every detail. We had to make a list to be sure that there wasn't any contraband in there-- money, et cetera. Now, I had what was called a [GERMAN], a slide rule from engineering school, something that the computer, calculator has long superseded. But I took the cardboard container apart. And I stuffed $20 in it. That was clandestine, trading foreign money in Vienna all the time, and particularly in those days. It's the old story for money you can get anything, almost. And so I stuffed the $20 in. And that's what I had when I came here. That's what the three of us had when we came here. However, my parents had bought a passage on a liner-- Paris. Second class, I want you to know, which was a tremendous thing. And we stopped in Paris. And my brother, the good-looking guy that he was, went up to the Joint Committee and gave them a nice story. And they gave us some francs. And we stayed a whole week in Paris. And we went to the Folies Bergere. [LAUGHS] And we really savored that freedom like I can't tell you. It was like-- From there, we went to the harbor-- my brother, my sister, and I. It wasn't that simple. My father took us to the railroad station. The three of us got on to the train. We looked out the window. And this man ran with the train-- and waving goodbye until the end of the-- [CRYING] And that-- those things stay in your mind. It's not so easy to send three children away. My mother stayed home. She couldn't handle it. And my father subsequently had a stroke. But he didn't die from it. He was taken to the camp anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken to Theresienstadt in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well there's a letter, a little postcard here from my mother from Theresienstadt and also letters from my mother, in which she says that my father is suffering badly, and that she tries to limit his pain, but that he's very sensitive, et cetera, et cetera. And he passed away in Theresienstadt, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of normal causes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- I, I know that they lived in Vienna for several years. It must have been in '43. And we went to America. We landed in New York. A relative had sent somebody to pick us up, put us into a rooming house for one night on the west side. I marveled at the big sign of American Biscuit. I couldn't get over it, how big a company can be that makes biscuits. Little did I know or understand then. And my sister and brother continued on to Chicago because their affidavit people were in Chicago. And I stayed here in New York. And I went to the hires because they told me they had jobs for refugees. And they had a soup kitchen there. And I ate soup there twice a day for three days. And they got me a job in New Brunswick, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a battery factory as a maintenance man. And I was elated. This new freedom that I had, it was just wonderful, I must say. I had to get adjusted to everything. I lived in a tiny room. It was winter. I had the milk and the cereal outside. I latched on quickly to American cereal. It was our breakfast. And, and that's how it all started here. And, of course, I received desperate letters from my parents. And I, I must say that it's been a lifelong guilt feeling for me that I couldn't do-- I couldn't do anything, is the term. I couldn't do more than I did. It's really improper because summa summarum, I didn't do anything. I got them affidavits for my relatives. They sent for more papers. I got more papers. I saved every penny I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, the-- by that time, I had an engineering job. The head of the company, a Mr. [? Sadwith, ?] called me in and said, Charles, where do you live from? I said, Mr. [? Sadwith, ?] what do you mean? He says, well you haven't cashed any checks for eight weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Sir, I'm saving money to bring my parents over. He said, I thought you were trying to buy the company. He was joking. I deposited some $900 for their passage, which I was later refunded because the committee said it is useless to them. And so life has its ways of evening things out. I bought myself a car and had a near-fatal car accident. But that has nothing-- I, I don't look at life that way. One of those things that can happen. I couldn't. I just couldn't do anything for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to-- I have to interject for a moment. You, you were in a unique position, having been in Europe, having suffered under Hitler-- this was 1938. You came to America. You were in Dachau, in a Dachau concentration camp. You came to America. Did you tell anyone your story? Did you ask anyone to listen to what you were saying? And I'm curious what was the reaction of your relatives and of people you spoke to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my relatives had been aware of this for some time. They were the very ones who sent the affidavits for us. But their attitude was once you boys get established here and can support your parents, you'll be able to send for them. They didn't realize the urgency of it. And then when I showed them-- this relative in New York, who was rather well-to-do-- and I showed him these desperate letters of my mother. It was then that he issued a full affidavit to my mother. But it too late. There was no way that they would let them out by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That affidavit was already in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out what happened to, to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I had been in touch with various Jewish organizations that search in these matters. And I finally got a telegram from an organization in Prague that my mother had-- was not under those who had returned. There was only one person from Vienna who had returned. And [CRYING] having received the mail from Theresienstadt over this relative in Vienna, my brother's mother-in-law who was Gentile, I knew that they were in Theresienstadt. And I also got one of the other letter from her from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew where they were. And when I got this telegram, I naturally tried to dig into it more. But little by little, the realization grew on me that it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, as to co-workers, et cetera, engineering is not a Jewish game. The people I worked with were really not interested in what I had to say. To go so far, I had a-- I became chief engineer in short order. And I had a gentleman work for me who told me in so many words that I was the first Jew he ever met. He was from the far west. And he said he was surprised that I didn't wear a hat because his parents told him that all Jews have horns. Little did I know about Michelangelo-- Moses that is shown with horns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did this take place? This was in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in, in New Brunswick--

speaker: interviewer
text: In New Brunswick.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the job. And so there were others who were very nice. But there was little, little occasion, really, to dwell on this, I must say. I wasn't asked. I didn't volunteer. My bosses even didn't ask. And they were Jewish. They just watched my progress professionally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there came a time when I met my wife here in New York, even though she's from Vienna. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: --we fell in love.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a friend of mine was going to a dance. I had a cousin here in New York who had married a doctor, Dr. Eugene [? Seeley. ?] And I visited them regularly, became real friends. She was a wonderful girl. I had stayed at her parents' house in Czechoslovakia. So there was more to it than just that. And one day, my friend was at a dance. And I went to pick him up with my car. And he had met this girl. And her friend was my wife. And when she came down, he introduced me to her. And one word to another, and we used the Viennese slang word. And that, of course, having been away from this so long, that really paved the way. And she cancelled her other dates. And we went out for dinner. And then I met her parents. And sooner or later, we decided to get married and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] married in New Brunswick. And I asked my boss for a raise. I asked for a hundred dollars more.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married? What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. He-- I, I asked for a hundred dollars. And he said, we have to think about it. And he came back and said, we can only afford $50. And in my mind, I said, I can't afford to stay here much longer. And in two weeks, I had another job in Indiana. I was hired by a patent attorney for the company he had. Mr. Austin was a member of the law firm of Austin and Seabury. Seabury was very famous in a New York investigation many years ago, a very prestigious firm. And here I was, shipped out to Indiana. And I was told he knows that I'm a Jew. But I'm going to meet very few Jews out there and that I should try to integrate. And the fact of the matter was that I was the only Jew in this large company. It's a worldwide company that had a factory in Australia, another factory in England. It was started by Germans. And of course, it was during the war. We were building war material. I was deferred because I was starting a new division, managing a new division for them. And there was no time to dwell on anything else. We worked from morning till late at night. It was my wife who joined the Jewry. We had a relative in South Bend. The daughter of this Sabbath had married the local district attorney, whose name was [? Shear. ?] And he paved the way for my wife to join card games, and-- et cetera. My wife also worked bandages for the Red Cross. She spent her time that way. But my time was totally absorbed in organizing this new enterprise for them as a division and then designing, building machinery using the engineers. And so I had no time to think about it. But just as a little aside, I got along reasonably well. The chief engineer was Mr. [? Turnberg. ?] There was another assistant chief engineer who said, I just got a call from downstairs. They say to send the kike down that we have up here now. But just because a guy has black hair, he doesn't have to be Jewish. I will admit that I passed for one year there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, it was too much. There were conversations in the engineering department continuously about kikes, et cetera. And finally I said, look, fellas. You know I'm a Jew. Why do you keep talking like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charlie, you're a white Jew. You're not like the others. Gee, I, I always went Yom Kippur to New York-- so I wouldn't go through the stands out there-- with my wife to visit her parents. They were living in New York. And the chief engineer, Roy [? Guide, ?] a brilliant man-- I want to show you how his brilliance has nothing to do with the, the inside, the soul of people, nothing. Brilliance is an intellectual pursuit. It's nothing to do with their feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Roy would say, Charlie, what do you want to do? And what is there in New York? What do you want to do there? There's nothing but Jews and niggers. I said, Roy, you've never been to New York. There's Central Park. We got parks here in South Bend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, personally, I would have stayed. I loved the work, I loved the engineers I worked with. And I felt it was an opportunity for me there. My wife couldn't take it. She felt the atmosphere oppressive. She felt it, I'm going to admit, small townish. The fact that it was not in South Bend. The fact that it was in Mishawaka, a small community. Well, there was several other big factories there, but predominantly one of the small factory towns. I had very little contact with the upper hierarchy in the company. I was recognized as the manager of the division. I went to meetings with them. But socially, I've never been invited in any one of their homes. Roy [? Guide, ?] the very chief engineer, after we had our first son, Howard, came to the house with a creche as an offering for the new baby. I wasn't there. I was in a movie. [LAUGHS] My wife was very hard-pressed what to do. He came with his wife. He stayed there for a short while. Then he left. It was the only personal contact that I ever had with. Oh, I played tennis with one of the other draftsman who-- Phil [? Johnson, ?] who was from Denver, Colorado. My personal secretary was an Italian woman, Anna Maria [? Frisoli. ?] She was extremely attuned to this because she was first-generation American. Her father was an immigrant with a heavy Italian accent. So she didn't object to mine. But in those days, even that contact was nominal because we didn't dictate letters. We used the old Edison wax roll to dictate letters. It was a large company. It went to the steno pool. Then we got the copies, we corrected them, and sent them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a different style of doing business in those days. But I was happy there, I must say, professionally. But when my wife, her sister didn't-- talking about missing her parents, and her sister, and brother-in-law, who had come in the meantime from France and Portugal. I succumbed and got myself another job. We'll continue on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly.
